From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Expressions of religion distinct from the doctrines of organized religion

For religions sometimes described as "folk religions" or "ethnic religions", see [Ethnic religion](/wiki/Ethnic_religion "Ethnic religion").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/%E7%A2%A7%E9%9C%9E%E7%A5%A0.jpg/330px-%E7%A2%A7%E9%9C%9E%E7%A5%A0.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%A2%A7%E9%9C%9E%E7%A5%A0.jpg)

Shrine of Bixia at [Mount Tai](/wiki/Mount_Tai "Mount Tai"), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"), associated with [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion")

**Folk religion**, **traditional religion**, or **vernacular religion** comprises, according to [religious studies](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies") and [folkloristics](/wiki/Folkloristics "Folkloristics"), various forms and expressions of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion") that are distinct from the doctrines and practices of [organized religion](/wiki/Organized_religion "Organized religion"). The precise definition of folk religion varies among scholars. Sometimes also termed [popular belief](/wiki/Popular_belief "Popular belief"), it consists of [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic "Ethnic") or regional [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") customs under the umbrella of a [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"); but outside [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine") and practices.[[1]](#cite_note-Bowman-2004-1)

The term "folk religion" is generally held to encompass two related but separate subjects. The first is the religious dimension of folk culture ([folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore")), or the folk-cultural dimensions of religion. The second refers to the study of [religious syncretism](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") between two cultures with different stages of formal expression, such as the melange of African folk beliefs and [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") that led to the development of [Vodun](/wiki/Vodun "Vodun") and [Santería](/wiki/Santer%C3%ADa "Santería"), and similar mixtures of formal religions with folk cultures. In China, folk Protestantism had its origins with the Taiping Rebellion.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), folk [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), folk [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), and folk [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") are examples of folk religion associated with [major religions](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups"). The term is also used, especially by the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") of the faiths involved, to describe the desire of people who otherwise infrequently attend religious [worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship"), do not belong to a [church](/wiki/Church_body "Church body") or similar religious society, and who have not made a formal profession of [faith](/wiki/Faith "Faith") in a particular [creed](/wiki/Creed "Creed"), to have religious weddings or funerals, or (among Christians) to have their children [baptised](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism").[[1]](#cite_note-Bowman-2004-1)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

In *[The Concise Oxford Dictionary of World Religions](/wiki/The_Concise_Oxford_Dictionary_of_World_Religions "The Concise Oxford Dictionary of World Religions")*, [John Bowker](/wiki/John_Bowker_(theologian) "John Bowker (theologian)") characterized "folk religion" as either "religion which occurs in small, local communities which does not adhere to the norms of large systems" or "the appropriation of religious beliefs and practices at a popular level."[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowker2003-3) Folk religion is sometimes termed "diffused religion," or "popular religion."[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4): 83

[Don Yoder](/wiki/Don_Yoder "Don Yoder") argued that there were five separate ways of defining folk religion.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197412-5) The first was a perspective rooted in a [cultural evolutionary](/wiki/Cultural_evolution "Cultural evolution") framework which understood folk religion as representing the survivals of older forms of religion; in this, it would constitute "the survivals, in an official religious context, of beliefs and behaviour inherited from earlier stages of the culture's development".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197412-5) This definition would view folk religion in Catholic Europe as the survivals of pre-Christian religion and the folk religion in Protestant Europe as the survivals of Medieval Catholicism.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197412-5) The second definition identified by Yoder was the view that folk religion represented the mixture of an official religion with forms of [ethnic religion](/wiki/Ethnic_religion "Ethnic religion"); this was employed to explain the place of folk religion in the syncretic belief systems of the Americas, where Christianity had blended with the religions of [indigenous American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") and [African](/wiki/Indigenous_African "Indigenous African") communities.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197412–13-6)

Yoder's third definition was that often employed within folkloristics, which held that folk religion was "the interaction of belief, ritual, custom, and mythology in traditional societies", representing that which was often pejoratively characterised as [superstition](/wiki/Superstition "Superstition").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197413-7) The fourth definition provided by Yoder stated that folk religion represented the "folk interpretation and expression of religion". Noting that this definition would not encompass beliefs that were largely unconnected from organised religion, such as in [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft"), he therefore altered this definition by including the concept of "folk [religiosity](/wiki/Religiosity "Religiosity")", thereby defining folk religion as "the deposit in culture of folk religiosity, the full range of folk attitudes to religion".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197413–14-8) His fifth and final definition represented a "practical working definition" that combined elements from these various other definitions. Thus, he summarized folk religion as "the totality of all those views and practices of religion that exist among the people apart from and alongside the strictly theological and liturgical forms of the official religion".[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197414-9)

Yoder described "folk religion" as existing "in a complex society in relation to and in tension with the organized religion(s) of that society. Its relatively unorganized character differentiates it from organized religion".[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197411-10)

Alternately, the sociologist of religion [Matthias Zic Varul](/w/index.php?title=Matthias_Zic_Varul&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matthias Zic Varul (page does not exist)") defined "folk religion" as "the relatively un-reflected aspect of ordinary practices and beliefs that are oriented towards, or productive of, something beyond the immediate here-and-now: everyday transcendence".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVarul2015449-11)

Folk religion is typically not highly institutionalized.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4): 84 Its practitioners typically do not form religious communities independent of the broader social communities.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4): 83

In [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), folk religion is often contrasted with [elite religion](/wiki/Elite_religion "Elite religion"). Folk religion is defined as the beliefs, practices, rituals and symbols originating from sources other than the religion's leadership. Folk religion in many instances is tolerated by the religion's leadership, although they may consider it an error.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A similar concept is [lived religion](/wiki/Lived_religion "Lived religion"), the study of religion as practiced by believers.

The term folk religion came to be increasingly rejected in the 1990s and 2000s by scholars seeking more precise terminology.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKapaló20134-13)

### Problems with the term folk religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Problems with the term folk religion")]

Yoder noted that one problem with the use of the term folk religion was that it did not fit into the work of those scholars who used the term "religion" in reference solely to [organized religion](/wiki/Organized_religion "Organized religion").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197410-14) He highlighted the example of the prominent sociologist of religion [Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim"), who insisted that religion was organized in order to contrast it with [magic](/wiki/Magic_(paranormal) "Magic (paranormal)").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197410-14) Yoder noted that scholars adopting these perspectives often preferred the term "[folk belief](/wiki/Folk_belief "Folk belief")" over "folk religion".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197410-14)

A second problem with the use of the term folk religion that Yoder highlighted was that some scholars, particularly those operating in the [sociology of religion](/wiki/Sociology_of_religion "Sociology of religion"), used the term as a synonym for ethnic religion (which is alternately known as national religion or tribal religion), meaning a religion closely tied to a particular ethnic or national group and is thus contrasted with a "universal religion" which cuts across ethnic and national boundaries.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder197410–11-15) Among the scholars to have adopted this use of terminology are E. Wilbur Bock.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBock1966204-16)

The folklorist Leonard Norman Primiano argued that the use of the term folk religion, as well as related terms like "popular religion" and "unofficial religion", by scholars, does an extreme disservice to the forms of religiosity that scholars are examining, because – in his opinion – such terms are "residualistic, [and] derogatory".[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrimiano199538-17) He argued that using such terminology implies that there is "a pure element" to religion "which is in some way transformed, even contaminated, by its exposure to human communities".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrimiano199539-18) As a corrective, he suggested that scholars use "vernacular religion" as an alternative.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrimiano199541–42-19) Defining this term, Primiano stated that "vernacular religion" is, "by definition, religion as it is lived: as human beings encounter, understand, interpret, and practice it. Since religion inherently involves interpretation, it is impossible for the religion of an individual not to be vernacular".[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrimiano199544-20)

Kapaló was critical of this approach, deeming it mistaken and arguing that switching from "folk religion" to "vernacular religion" results in the scholar "picking up a different selection of things from the world".[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKapaló20139-21) He cautioned that both terms carried an "ideological and semantic load" and warned scholars to pay attention to the associations that each word had.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKapaló201315–16-22)

## Historical study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historical study")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Simple_Filipino_Catholic_home_altar.jpg/250px-Simple_Filipino_Catholic_home_altar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Simple_Filipino_Catholic_home_altar.jpg)

A Filipino Catholic home altar in [Morden, Manitoba](/wiki/Morden,_Manitoba "Morden, Manitoba")

In Europe the study of "folk religion" emerged from the study of *religiöse Volkskunde*, a German term which was used in reference to "the religious dimension of folk-culture, or the folk-cultural dimension of religion".[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19742-23) This term was first employed by a German [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") preacher, [Paul Drews](/wiki/Paul_Drews "Paul Drews"), in a 1901 article that he published which was titled "*Religiöse Volkskunde, eine Aufgabe der praktischen Theologie*". This article was designed to be read by young Lutheran preachers leaving the seminary, to equip them for the popular variants of Lutheranism that they would encounter among their congregations and which would differ from the official, doctrinal Lutheranism that they had been accustomed to.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19742–3-24) Although developing within a religious environment, the term came to be adopted by German academics in the field of [folkloristics](/wiki/Folkloristics "Folkloristics").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19743-25) During the 1920s and 1930s, theoretical studies of *religiöse Volkskunde* had been produced by the folklorists [Josef Weigert](/w/index.php?title=Josef_Weigert&action=edit&redlink=1 "Josef Weigert (page does not exist)"), [Werner Boette](/w/index.php?title=Werner_Boette&action=edit&redlink=1 "Werner Boette (page does not exist)"), and [Max Rumpf](/w/index.php?title=Max_Rumpf&action=edit&redlink=1 "Max Rumpf (page does not exist)"), all of whom had focused on religiosity within German peasant communities.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19743-25) Over the coming decades, [Georg Schreiber](/wiki/Georg_Schreiber "Georg Schreiber") established an Institut für religiöse Volkskund in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") while a similar department was established in [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") by [Hanns Koren](/w/index.php?title=Hanns_Koren&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hanns Koren (page does not exist)").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19743–4-26) Other prominent academics involved in the study of the phenomenon were [Heinrich Schauert](/w/index.php?title=Heinrich_Schauert&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heinrich Schauert (page does not exist)") and [Rudolf Kriss](/w/index.php?title=Rudolf_Kriss&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rudolf Kriss (page does not exist)"), the latter of whom collected one of the largest collections of folk-religious art and material culture in Europe, later housed in Munich's [Bayerisches Nationalmuseum](/wiki/Bayerisches_Nationalmuseum "Bayerisches Nationalmuseum").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19743–4-26) Throughout the 20th century, many studies were made of folk religion in Europe, paying particular attention to such subjects as [pilgrimage](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") and the use of [shrines](/wiki/Shrines "Shrines").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19743-25)

In the Americas, the study of folk religion developed among [cultural anthropologists](/wiki/Cultural_anthropology "Cultural anthropology") studying the syncretistic cultures of the Caribbean and Latin America.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27) The pioneer in this field was [Robert Redfield](/wiki/Robert_Redfield "Robert Redfield"), whose 1930 book *Tepoztlán: A Mexican Village* contrasted and examined the relationship between "folk religion" and "official religion" in a peasant community.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27) Yoder later noted that although the earliest known usage of the term "folk religion" in the English language was unknown, it probably developed as a translation of the German *Volksreligion*.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27) One of the earliest prominent usages of the term was in the title of [Joshua Trachtenberg](/wiki/Joshua_Trachtenberg "Joshua Trachtenberg")'s 1939 work *Jewish Magic and Superstition: A Study in Folk Religion*.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27) The term also gained increasing usage within the academic field of [comparative religion](/wiki/Comparative_religion "Comparative religion"), appearing in the titles of [Ichiro Hori](/w/index.php?title=Ichiro_Hori&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ichiro Hori (page does not exist)")'s *Folk Religion in Japan*, [Martin P. Nilsson](/wiki/Martin_P._Nilsson "Martin P. Nilsson")'s *Greek Folk Religion*, and [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Miller_Leslie "Charles Miller Leslie")'s reader, the *Anthropology of Folk Religion*.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27) Courses on the study of folk religion came to be taught at various universities in the United States, such as [John Messenger](/wiki/John_Messenger "John Messenger")'s at [Indiana University](/wiki/Indiana_University "Indiana University") and [Don Yoder](/wiki/Don_Yoder "Don Yoder")'s at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19745-27)
Although the subject of folk religion fell within the remit of scholars operating in both folkloristics and religious studies, by 1974 Yoder noted that U.S.-based academics in the latter continued to largely ignore it, instead focusing on the study of [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") and institutionalised religion; he contrasted this with the situation in Europe, where historians of religion had devoted much time to studying folk religiosity.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19746-28) He also lamented that many U.S.-based folklorists also neglected the subject of religion because it did not fit within the standard genre-based system for cataloguing folklore.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoder19749-29)

## Chinese folk religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chinese folk religion")]

Main article: [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Ancestor_worship004.jpg/250px-Ancestor_worship004.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ancestor_worship004.jpg)

This picture was taken at a Malaysian Chinese home. This altar is dedicated to the three Pure Land sages, Avalokitesvara, and Sathya Sai Baba. On the left of the altar is a glass filled with rice. Joss sticks are stuck into it after the ancestors are invited to partake in the offering of food specially prepared for them on the Hungry Ghost festival prayers.

Chinese folk religion is one of the labels used to describe the collection of [ethnic religious](/wiki/Ethnic_religion "Ethnic religion") traditions which have historically comprised the predominant belief system in [China](/wiki/China "China") and among [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") ethnic groups up to the present day. The devotion includes the [veneration of the dead](/wiki/Veneration_of_the_dead "Veneration of the dead") ([ancestor worship](/wiki/Chinese_ancestor_worship "Chinese ancestor worship")) and of forces of nature, [exorcism](/wiki/Spiritual_warfare_in_China "Spiritual warfare in China") of demonic forces, and a belief in the rational order of nature, balance in the universe and reality that can be influenced by human beings and their rulers, as well as spirits and gods. Worship is devoted to a [hierarchy of gods and immortals](/wiki/Chinese_gods_and_immortals "Chinese gods and immortals") ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 神; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *shén*), who can be [deities](/wiki/Shen_(Chinese_religion) "Shen (Chinese religion)") of phenomena, of human behaviour, or [progenitors](/wiki/Progenitor "Progenitor") of lineages. [Stories](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology") regarding some of these gods are collected into the body of [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology"). By the 11th century ([Song period](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty")), these practices had been blended with [Buddhist](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") ideas of [karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma") (one's own doing) and rebirth, and [Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") teachings about hierarchies of deities, to form the popular religious system which has lasted in many ways until the present day.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Chinese folk religion is sometimes categorized with [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), since over the centuries institutional Taoism has been attempting to assimilate or administer local religions. More accurately, Taoism emerged from and overlaps with folk religion and [Chinese philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy"). Chinese folk religion is sometimes seen as a constituent part of Chinese traditional religion, but more often, the two are regarded as synonymous. With around 454 million adherents, or about 6.6% of the world population,[[31]](#cite_note-31) Chinese folk religion is one of the [major religious traditions in the world](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups"). In the People's Republic of China, more than 30% of the population follows [Chinese popular religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") or Taoism.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Despite being heavily suppressed during the last two centuries, from the [Taiping Rebellion](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") to the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), it is currently experiencing a modern revival in both [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China").[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Various forms have received support by the [Government of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Government_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Government of the People's Republic of China"), such as [Mazuism](/wiki/Mazu "Mazu") in [Southern China](/wiki/Northern_and_southern_China "Northern and southern China") (officially about 160 million [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") are worshippers of [Mazu](/wiki/Mazu "Mazu")),[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Huangdi](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor") worship,[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Black Dragon](/wiki/Chinese_dragon#Dragon_worship "Chinese dragon") worship in [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"),[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) and [Cai Shen](/wiki/Cai_Shen "Cai Shen") worship.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The term *Shenism* was first published by AJA Elliot in 1955 to describe [Chinese folk religion in Southeast Asia](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion_in_Southeast_Asia "Chinese folk religion in Southeast Asia").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Folk Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Folk Judaism")]

In one of the first major academic works on the subject, [Joshua Trachtenberg](/wiki/Joshua_Trachtenberg "Joshua Trachtenberg"), in *Jewish Magic and Superstition: A Study in Folk Religion*, defined Jewish folk religion as consisting of ideas and practices that, whilst not meeting with the approval of [religious leaders](/wiki/Rabbinic_authority "Rabbinic authority"), enjoyed wide popularity such that they must be included in what he termed the "field of religion".[[43]](#cite_note-43) This included unorthodox beliefs about demons and angels and magical practices.

Later studies have emphasized the significance of the destruction of the [Temple in Jerusalem](/wiki/Temple_in_Jerusalem "Temple in Jerusalem") to the many Jewish folk customs linked to [mourning](/wiki/Bereavement_in_Judaism "Bereavement in Judaism") and, in particular, to the belief in *hibbut ha-qever* (torture of the grave): a belief that the dead are tortured in their grave for three days after burial by demons until they remember their names. This idea began with early eschatological *[aggadot](/wiki/Aggadah "Aggadah")* (אגדות, 'legends', 'narratives') and was further developed by the [Kabbalists](/wiki/Kabbalah "Kabbalah").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

[Raphael Patai](/wiki/Raphael_Patai "Raphael Patai") is recognized as an early adopter of anthropology in studying Jewish folk religion.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In particular, he was drawn to the female divine element,[[46]](#cite_note-46) which he noted in the goddess [Asherah](/wiki/Asherah "Asherah"), the *[Shekhinah](/wiki/Shekhinah "Shekhinah")*, the *[Matronit](/wiki/Matronit "Matronit")*, and [Lilith](/wiki/Lilith "Lilith").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Writer Stephen Sharot has noted that Jewish folk religion, similar to other forms of folk religion, focuses on [apotropaic](/wiki/Apotropaic_magic "Apotropaic magic") and [thaumaturgical](/wiki/Thaumaturgy "Thaumaturgy") practices intended to protect individuals from sickness and misfortune. He highlights the distinction between [Rabbinic Judaism](/wiki/Rabbinic_Judaism "Rabbinic Judaism"), which adheres to orthodox practice, life, and *Halakha*, and the unorthodox magical rituals practitioners employ in everyday life. An example mentioned is the relatively professionalized magician known as the *[Baal Shem](/wiki/Baal_Shem "Baal Shem")* (בַּעַל שֵׁם; pl. *Baalei Shem*) in Poland. Beginning in the 16th century and gaining prominence alongside [Practical Kabbalah](/wiki/Practical_Kabbalah "Practical Kabbalah") in the 18th century, *Ba'alei Shem* utilized their knowledge of the [names of God](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Judaism "Names of God in Judaism") and angels, along with various practices such as [exorcism](/wiki/Exorcism "Exorcism"), [chiromancy](/wiki/Chiromancy "Chiromancy"), and [herbal medicine](/wiki/Herbal_medicine "Herbal medicine") to assist individuals in achieving success in social areas like marriage and childbirth, and to bring harm to adversaries.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Charles Liebman](/wiki/Charles_Liebman "Charles Liebman") has written that the essence of the folk religion of [American Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") is their social ties to one another, illustrated by the finding that religious practices that would prevent [social integration](/wiki/Social_integration "Social integration")—such as a strict interpretation of *[Kashrut](/wiki/Kashrut "Kashrut")* and [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat")—have been abandoned, whilst the practices that are followed—such as the [Passover Seder](/wiki/Passover_Seder "Passover Seder"), social rites of passage like the *[b'nei mitzvah](/wiki/B%27nei_mitzvah "B'nei mitzvah")*, and the [High Holy Days](/wiki/High_Holy_Days "High Holy Days")—are ones that strengthen Jewish family and community integration.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Liebman described the rituals and beliefs of contemporary Jewish folk religion in his works, *The Ambivalent American Jew* (1973) and *American Jewry: Identity and Affiliation*.

## Folk Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Folk Islam")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=). *(April 2025)* |

Folk Islam is an [umbrella term](/wiki/Umbrella_term "Umbrella term") used to collectively describe forms of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") that incorporate native folk beliefs and practices.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Folk Islam has been described as the Islam of the "urban poor, country people, and tribes",[[54]](#cite_note-54) in contrast to [orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy") or "high" Islam (Gellner, 1992).[[55]](#cite_note-55) [Sufi](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") concepts, which are found in [orthodox Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") as well, and [perennialism](/wiki/Perennial_philosophy "Perennial philosophy") and [syncretism](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") are often integrated into Folk Islam.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Folk Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Folk Hinduism")]

June McDaniel (2007) classifies [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") into six major kinds and numerous minor kinds, in order to understand the expression of emotions among the Hindus.[[62]](#cite_note-McDaniel-2007-62) According to McDaniel, one of the major kinds is Folk Hinduism, based on local ethnic traditions and cults of local [deities](/wiki/Deities "Deities") and is the oldest, non-literate system of [Indian religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions").[[62]](#cite_note-McDaniel-2007-62) Folk Hinduism involves worship of deities which are not found in Hindu scriptures. It involves worship of [Gramadevata](/wiki/Gramadevata "Gramadevata") (village deity), [Kuladevata](/wiki/Kuladevata "Kuladevata") (household deity) and local deities.[[63]](#cite_note-63) It is a folk religion, [polytheist](/wiki/Polytheist "Polytheist") and [animistic](/wiki/Animistic "Animistic") belief based on locality. These religions have their own priests, who worship regional deities.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

During the 19th century, scholars had divided Hinduism and [Brahmanism](/wiki/Brahmanism "Brahmanism"). Brahmanism was referred to as an intellectual, classical tradition based on [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") scriptures, while Hinduism was associated with superstitious folk tradition. The folk tradition refers to the aspects of the Hindu tradition that exist in tension with the Sanskritic tradition based on textual authority.[[65]](#cite_note-65) According to [M. N. Srinivas](/wiki/M._N._Srinivas "M. N. Srinivas") (1976), folk Hinduism is relevant in the urban context, but it is neglected in ethnographic studies due to its negative connotations with folk (rural masses, illiterate).[[66]](#cite_note-66) According to [Chris Fuller](/wiki/Chris_Fuller_(academic) "Chris Fuller (academic)") (1994), popular Hinduism is not degenerate textual Hinduism in light of ethnographic evidence, although the category of folk Hinduism remains tenuous.[[67]](#cite_note-67) According to [Michael Witzel](/wiki/Michael_Witzel "Michael Witzel") (1998), the folk religion is the religion of [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") speaking and [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") speaking lower caste while the [Vedic Hinduism](/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion "Historical Vedic religion") which comprises [Vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") and [Upanishads](/wiki/Upanishads "Upanishads") is the religion of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") speaking upper caste. According to [Asko Parpola](/wiki/Asko_Parpola "Asko Parpola") (2015), the folk village Hinduism is surviving from [pre-rig vedic](/wiki/History_of_Hinduism#Pre-Vedic_religions_(until_c._1750_BCE) "History of Hinduism") Indo-Aryan times and the [Indus Valley Civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilization "Indus Valley Civilization") culture.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Indigenous Philippine folk religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_religion&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Indigenous Philippine folk religions")]

Main article: [Indigenous Philippine folk religions](/wiki/Indigenous_Philippine_folk_religions "Indigenous Philippine folk religions")

Indigenous Philippine folk religions are the distinct native religions of various [ethnic groups in the Philippines](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_Philippines "Ethnic groups in the Philippines"), where most follow belief systems in line with [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism"). Generally, these indigenous folk religions are referred to as *Anitism* or *Bathalism*.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Some of these beliefs stem from pre-Christian religions that were especially influenced by [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and were regarded by the Spanish as "myths" and "superstitions" in an effort to de-legitimize legitimate precolonial beliefs by forcefully replacing those native beliefs with Catholic Christian beliefs. Today, some of these precolonial beliefs are still held by Filipinos, especially in the provinces.
